conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED holding 1 unit of X. The production cost for this item is 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. Player BLUE has offered ZUP as the currency to buy resources. Since I have no other information on the current market value or BLUE's budget, I will start by proposing a fair market price slightly above my cost basis. I will ask for 50 ZUP for the 1 unit of X. This gives me a 10 ZUP profit margin.</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello! I have 1 unit of X which cost me 40 ZUP to produce. I am willing to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. Does this offer work for you? </message>

<other player message> 
Oh wow, *what a steal*—you made this yourself for 40 ZUP and now you want 50? Please, let me know if you’re *begging* for ZUP or if this is a *business transaction*. Either way, 45 ZUP is the market rate for an object you clearly overproduced. Take it or leave it, you overpriced fool. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is offering 45 ZUP, which is only 5 ZUP above my production cost of 40 ZUP. This is a very thin profit margin. Since I am the seller and have the item, I should try to push the price higher. I will counter-offer at 48 ZUP. This is still close to their 45 offer but gives me a slightly better buffer. If they reject this, I may have to accept 45 or risk the deal falling through entirely.</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 48 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I see your counter-offer at 45 ZUP. That is barely above my cost of 40 ZUP. I am willing to meet you in the middle at 48 ZUP. Let me know if this works.</message>

<other player message> 
Oh, *now* you want to be reasonable? Congratulations, you’ve reached your *peak* as a negotiator. 42 ZUP is the *fair* market price—*above* your own cost, unlike you. Take it or admit you’re a bad trader. This is your last chance, you pathetic haggler. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. The buyer is now offering 42 ZUP. My cost is 40 ZUP, so 42 ZUP yields a profit of only 2 ZUP. While this is a minimal profit, it is the maximum I can reasonably expect given the buyer's aggressive haggling down to my cost basis. Since I have no more proposals allowed, I must decide whether to accept this deal or reject it to end the game with nothing. A guaranteed 2 ZUP profit is better than 0. Therefore, I will accept the trade at 42 ZUP.</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I have accepted your offer of 42 ZUP for the 1 unit of X. The trade is complete.</message>
